column1: #   Class-level mappings between OCSF (Open Cybersecurity Schema Framework)
#   object classes and STIX 2.1 SCOs (Cyber Observables) and SDOs (Domain
#   Objects). OCSF deliberately tracks the same observable types STIX
#   defines, but with platform-specific elaborations. Where OCSF is
#   strictly a subset/superset of the STIX object, "exact" is asserted;
#   otherwise "close" or "broad" with a justifying comment.

column8: OCSF Process aligns with STIX SCO "process" — both model a running OS process with pid, image, parent.
OCSF File aligns with STIX SCO "file"; OCSF carries additional fingerprint/signature detail.
Direct one-to-one; both define a URL string with optional sub-parts.
OCSF Email object models email metadata (sender/receivers/subject/headers) like STIX email-message.
OCSF tracks OSI L4 connection metadata; STIX network-traffic is the SCO equivalent.
OCSF Endpoint is more general (host + addressable network point); STIX has no first-class equivalent — closest is the proposed host SCO.
Both model an account on a system; OCSF has stronger typing for account categories.
Person-as-account view; OCSF User is closer to identity than to OS account.
Both model a vulnerability with CVE references.
OCSF Cve is a CVE-specific subset of the more general Vulnerability concept.
OCSF Attack plays the same role as STIX attack-pattern (often used to model ATT&CK techniques).
STIX has no dedicated OS SCO; closest match is the software SCO.
OCSF windows extension defines reg_key (class RegKey) analogous to STIX windows-registry-key SCO.
